turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinions of the Court in two cases this morning.

turns.name: JUSTICE BARRETT
turns.text: This case is FDA versus R. J. Reynolds Vapor Company. The Family Smoking Prevention and Tobacco Control Act, which I will call the TCA for short, makes it illegal for companies to make or sell any new tobacco product unless the FDA first decides that the product is appropriate for the protection of the public health. When e-cigarettes first became popular in America, the FDA did not treat them as new tobacco products, so they were bought and sold without prior FDA approval. But in 2016, the FDA changed course, starting then, e-cigarrete manufacturers had to show that their products were appropriate for the protection of the public health or else the products would go off the market. R. J. Reynolds Vapor Company, I am going to call it RJR Vapor for short, sold multiple Vuse Alto branded e-cigarrete products, and it applied to the FDA for permission to keep selling them. The FDA denied those applications and if that denial order was upheld, it would take one of the most popular e-cigarrete brands off the market. RJR Vapor sued, arguing that the FDA was required to approve its applications but the case before us today is not about the substance of the FDA's denial order. It's instead about where the lawsuit about that denial order will happen. And as it turns out, where this lawsuit proceeds depends on who is allowed to challenge the order. The TCA allows any person "adversely affected" by one of the FDA's denial orders to challenge that order and either the US Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit or the US Court of Appeals where the challenger resides. This phrase, "adversely affected" is the key phrase in the statute so every time I say it, imagine there are quotation marks around it. There is no question that RJR Vapor was adversely affected by the denial order. After all, it made e-cigarettes and could no longer sell them. RJR Vapor is in North Carolina Company and North Carolina is located in the Fourth Circuit, so if RJR Vapor proceeded alone, it could challenge the FDA and the D.C. Circuit or the Fourth Circuit, but RJR Vapor decided to team up with retailers who sold its products in Texas and Mississippi, states that are both located in the Fifth Circuit. Acting jointly, RJR Vapor and the retailers filed there. The FDA cried foul, it asked the Fifth Circuit to either dismiss the challenge or send it to the D.C. Circuit or the Fourth Circuit. According to the FDA, the retailers were not adversely affected by a denial order and thus could not seek review of it. If the FDA was right about that, then RJR Vapor would need to bring its suit alone, and if RJR Vapor was alone, it could not stay in the Fifth Circuit. The Fifth Circuit disagreed with the FDA and kept the case. It held that the retailers were adversely affected by the FDA's refusal to authorize the sale of products that they wish to sell. We agree with the Fifth Circuit. Terms like adversely affected and aggrieved are familiar ones. Congress often uses variations of this phrase to describe who can bring a lawsuit. Most famously, this language is used in the Administrative Procedure Acts cause of action, and in that context, we have interpreted the phrase broadly as allowing any person to sue who is even arguably within the zone of interest that a statute intends to protect. The FDA argues that the APA is special and that here we should give adversely affected a narrower meaning. But our cases do not support this APA special approach. On the contrary, we have interpreted several statutes that use variations of the phrase adversely affected and we have held that the phrase reaches anyone who even arguably falls within the statute zone of interests. We see no reason to give the phrase a different meaning here, so anyone who is even arguably within the interests that the TCA seeks to protect may challenge the FDA's denial order. The retailers fit the bill. They want to sell Vuse Alto branded products and the FDA's denial order is the difference between they are having a lucrative business and facing the threat of criminal penalties for selling illegal, unauthorized tobacco products. That direct impact is more than enough to show that the retailers are adversely affected by the FDA's denial order. The FDA pushes back, claiming that Congress intended for only the manufacturer applicant, here that would be RJR Vapor, to be able to challenge a denial order, but the FDA's speculations about congressional purpose cannot be squared with the text that Congress actually enacted. The word "any" suggests that Congress wanted this cause of action to extend to multiple persons, not just one, and the phrase "person adversely affected" instead of say, party or applicant, suggests that Congress wanted to look beyond the people directly involved in the FDA proceeding. Indeed, Congress used the phrase "holder of the application" in a different provision of the TCA, allowing only holders of applications to challenge any order from the FDA that withdraws a prior approval given, and that meaningful variation shows that Congress had something else in mind when it said that any person adversely affected could file a challenge. For the first time before this Court, the FDA asks us to hold that even if the retailers could sue by themselves, RJR Vapor could not team up with them in the same lawsuit. No court has ever analyzed that question and it would be imprudent for us to be the first. We thus affirm the Fifth Circuit's order denying the FDA's motion to transfer venue. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Sotomayor joins.
